other_observers: None; Me, no others at this time, but Mark Peterson and Brandon Percival may have seen this bird the previous weekend.
duration: For a few seconds while flying and then about 30 seconds while perched
specific_location: The bird was in the locust grove on the west side of Neenoshe Reservoir. It was about 200 feet south of where CR E terminates in the north-south road bordering the tree belt on the west side of the reservoir. It was in the shrubs on the west side of the black locust trees.
description: Taiga subspecies  I first saw this bird flying away from me and the bright rufous on the tail and wings were obvious. By overall size and medium length tail, it appeared to be a large sparrow and not a thrasher. It soon appeared at the edge of a low shrub where I had a good look at the perched bird in the shade of the shrub. The top of crown appeared rufous, although most of the face around the eye was grayish. The eye was black. The auriculars immediately behind the eye were grayish, but immediately faded to rufous and the lower check was also rufous. The sides of the nape were gray. The upper mandible was mostly dark, particularly the culmen, and the lower bill was straw-colored. The bill was medium-sized for a sparrow. Below the bill there were strong rufous moustaschial streaks that extended into a 'necklace' below the throat which was whitish. The rufous streaking on the upper breast was very dense on the sides and upper breast and continued down the flanks as a series of closely connected splotches. The center of the belly was mostly whitish. The wings were rufous with two indistinct white wing bars and the primary tips were dark. The back seemed rufous and gray, but not seen well. The legs were shaded, but appeared light-colored. The tail was rufous as were the upper tail coverts.Behaviors: I first saw this bird flying low from one short deciduous shrub to other, where it disappeared. It then reappeared at the side of another deciduous shrub, where I had very good looks at the bird, even though it was shaded. Most of the leaves were off the shrub. It then flew out of sight and I did not follow it any further.Call: Not HeardPlumage: Adult
